title: Addition & Subtraction
objective: Students will explore addition & subtraction concepts including Addition and subtraction within 20, Fluent adding and subtracting within 10, What the equals sign means and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Addition and subtraction within 20? Let's find out together!
Add and subtract within 20 using strategies such as making ten, decomposing a number leading to ten, and using known facts
Let me show you something cool. Solve 8 + 6 using making ten: 8 + 2 + 4 = 14
If you needs to work out '8 + 6', can they split the 6 into 2 + 4, use the 2 to fill up to 10, then add the remaining 4 to get 14?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Addition and subtraction within 20: Solve 8 + 6 using making ten: 8 + 2 + 4 = 14 Solve 13 − 4 by decomposing: 13 − 3 − 1 = 9
Let's explore Fluent adding and subtracting within 10! Have you ever wondered about this?
Fluently add and subtract within 10
Time to try this out: Answer any addition fact within 10 quickly from memory
Can you answer sums like '7 + 3' or '9 − 4' almost instantly — without stopping to count on their fingers?
Great job! Today we learned about Fluent adding and subtracting within 10. Answer any addition fact within 10 quickly from memory Answer any subtraction fact within 10 quickly from memory
Let's discover the amazing world of What the equals sign means!
Understand the meaning of the equal sign as 'is the same as' and determine if equations are true or false
Let's practice together! Explain that 6 = 6 is true because both sides are the same
Does you understand that '=' means 'the same as', so they can tell you whether '4 + 3 = 8 − 1' is true or false?
Great job! Today we learned about What the equals sign means. Explain that 6 = 6 is true because both sides are the same Determine that 4 + 1 = 5 + 2 is false
Let's explore Adding within 100! Have you ever wondered about this?
Add within 100 using strategies based on place value, including adding a two-digit and one-digit number, and a two-digit and a multiple of 10
Let's practice together! Calculate 46 + 7 using place value (46 + 4 + 3 = 53)
Can you work out sums like '47 + 8' or '35 + 50' by thinking about the tens and ones separately rather than counting up one at a time?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Adding within 100: Calculate 46 + 7 using place value (46 + 4 + 3 = 53) Calculate 38 + 40 = 78 using tens understanding
Today we're going to learn about Addition in any order. Get ready for an adventure!
Understand and apply the commutative property of addition: addends can be added in any order
Let's practice together! Explain that 3 + 8 gives the same answer as 8 + 3
Does you know that 6 + 4 gives the same answer as 4 + 6, so they can always choose whichever order makes the adding easier?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Addition in any order. Explain that 3 + 8 gives the same answer as 8 + 3 Use commutativity to choose the larger number to count on from

lessons.title: Finding a missing number in addition

steps.content: Welcome to an exciting lesson about Finding a missing number in addition!
Understand subtraction as finding an unknown addend (e.g. 10 − 8 = ? is the same as 8 + ? = 10)
Let's practice together! Solve 10 − 8 by thinking 'what do I add to 8 to make 10?'
Can you see that '10 − 8 = ?' is the same as asking 'what do I add to 8 to reach 10?' — so they can use their adding knowledge to help with subtraction?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Finding a missing number in addition: Solve 10 − 8 by thinking 'what do I add to 8 to make 10?' Explain that subtraction can be thought of as a missing-addend problem
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Inverse: addition undoes subtraction!
Recognise and use the inverse relationship between addition and subtraction to check calculations and solve missing-number problems
Time to try this out: Check 15 + 7 = 22 by calculating 22 − 7 = 15
If you works out 13 + 5 = 18, can they immediately use that to say '18 − 5 = 13' — without having to work the subtraction out all over again?
Great job! Today we learned about Inverse: addition undoes subtraction. Check 15 + 7 = 22 by calculating 22 − 7 = 15 Use the inverse to solve: □ + 9 = 14, so □ = 14 − 9 = 5

lessons.title: Mental and written addition and subtraction

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of Mental and written addition and subtraction!
Solve addition and subtraction problems using mental and written methods, including problems involving numbers, quantities, and measures
Time to try this out: Solve a two-step problem: 'I had 35p, spent 12p, then found 5p. How much now?'
If you is given a problem involving lengths, weights, or prices, can they decide whether to add or subtract and carry out the calculation correctly?

Great job! Today we learned about Mental and written addition and subtraction. Solve a two-step problem: 'I had 35p, spent 12p, then found 5p. How much now?' Choose an appropriate method (mental or written) for a given problem
Today we're going to learn about Adding two two-digit numbers. Get ready for an adventure!
Add and subtract two two-digit numbers using concrete objects, pictorial representations, and mental methods
Let's practice together! Calculate 34 + 27 using base-ten blocks or column addition
Can you add or subtract two two-digit numbers — like '43 + 28' — using counters, a drawing, or working it out in their head?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Adding two two-digit numbers: Calculate 34 + 27 using base-ten blocks or column addition Calculate 63 − 28 using a number line or partitioning
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Mental addition and subtraction (age 6+)!
Add and subtract a two-digit number and ones mentally and using concrete/pictorial representations
Time to try this out: Calculate 36 + 7 = 43 using objects or mentally
Can you work out '36 + 7' or '42 − 5' in their head — keeping the tens in place and only adjusting the ones?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Mental addition and subtraction (age 6+). Calculate 36 + 7 = 43 using objects or mentally Calculate 52 − 4 = 48 using a number line or mentally
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Unknown in Addition & Subtraction!
Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers
Let's practice together! Solve 8 + ? = 11 and write 3
If you sees '? + 6 = 14' or '9 − ? = 4' written on paper, can they figure out what number goes in the gap?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Unknown in Addition & Subtraction. Solve 8 + ? = 11 and write 3 Solve 5 = □ − 3 and write 8
Let's explore Fluent addition and subtraction! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recall and use addition and subtraction facts to 20 fluently, and derive and use related facts up to 100
Let's practice together! Rapidly recall 7 + 8 = 15 and 15 − 8 = 7
Can you quickly recall facts like '9 + 7 = 16' and '16 − 7 = 9', and use those to help work out bigger sums like '90 + 70'?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Fluent addition and subtraction: Rapidly recall 7 + 8 = 15 and 15 − 8 = 7 Use 6 + 4 = 10 to derive 60 + 40 = 100
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Adding and subtracting tens mentally!
Add and subtract a two-digit number and tens mentally and using concrete/pictorial representations
Let's practice together! Calculate 45 + 30 = 75 mentally
Can you quickly work out '54 + 30' or '78 − 20' in their head — knowing that only the tens digit changes?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Adding and subtracting tens mentally. Calculate 45 + 30 = 75 mentally Calculate 82 − 40 = 42 mentally
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Subtracting multiples of 10!
Subtract multiples of 10 (10–90) from multiples of 10 using place value strategies
Time to try this out: Calculate 70 − 30 = 40
Can you quickly work out '80 − 50' or '70 − 40' in their head by thinking about how many tens are left?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Subtracting multiples of 10: Calculate 70 − 30 = 40 Use base-ten blocks to show 80 − 50 = 30
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Adding Three Small Numbers!
Add three one-digit numbers using strategies including looking for pairs that make 10
Time to try this out: Calculate 5 + 7 + 3 by first adding 7 + 3 = 10, then 5 + 10 = 15
If you needs to add 4 + 6 + 8, can they spot that 4 and 6 make 10 first, then add 8 to get 18 — rather than just adding in order from left to right?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Adding Three Small Numbers: Calculate 5 + 7 + 3 by first adding 7 + 3 = 10, then 5 + 10 = 15 Add any three single-digit numbers correctly
Today we're going to learn about Grouping numbers to add. Get ready for an adventure!
Understand and apply the associative property of addition: when adding three numbers, any two can be added first
Let's practice together! Solve 2 + 6 + 4 by first adding 6 + 4 = 10, then 2 + 10 = 12
If you needs to add 3 + 7 + 6, can they spot that adding 3 and 7 first (to make 10) is easier — rather than always working left to right?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Grouping numbers to add: Solve 2 + 6 + 4 by first adding 6 + 4 = 10, then 2 + 10 = 12 Explain that grouping addends differently gives the same total
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Addition and subtraction strategies!
Use counting on and counting back as strategies for addition and subtraction
Time to try this out: Add 8 + 3 by starting at 8 and counting on 3 (9, 10, 11)
If you needs to work out '8 + 5', can they start from 8 and count up five more — rather than starting from 1 every time?
Great job! Today we learned about Addition and subtraction strategies. Add 8 + 3 by starting at 8 and counting on 3 (9, 10, 11) Subtract 12 − 3 by counting back 3 from 12 (11, 10, 9)
